creator: viktor.stujber+freebsd-bugs_v4CCPfay@gmail.com
text:
FreeBSD hosts its files on its own Git repository. It also runs its own SVN repository, but that stopped receiving updates 20 months ago. The git repository is being mirrored to Github. Github offers a SVN bridge for its Git repositories. In my case, I was using it to get freebsd/freebsd-ports. The SVN bridge was always unstable (randomly giving timeouts and internal server errors) and required multiple retries to execute a query, but it worked. Until august 2022, when it fell apart completely.

I have contacted Github support, who said "It looks as though this repository may be large enough that it is stretching the limits of the SVNBridge tool. We're looking into it, but it's unlikely there will be a short term fix.". They tried a rebuild, but it only lasted a few queries before it broke again. It is 3 months later with no change.

I have posted about it in https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/28729 and got a few people who also observed this problem. The freebsd-ports repository currently has around 6m items and takes up 2GB. I don't know if this is considered too large, or if their tool simply doesn't scale.

I use a very space-constrained copy of the ports tree, relying on svn's --set-depth parameter to only include the ports I need. Overall, 200 ports take up 20MB of space, including the checkout. It is possible to achieve a similar result with Git, but it is much more complicated and error-prone. The cloned data needs to be obtained using a sequence of niche commands, and has to be re-pruned after every pull. It also needs to be re-cloned whenever a port is to be added or removed. Getting this to work would require a set of carefully written helper scripts, whereas with SVN it's straightforward single commands that can be typed out by hand. In addition, merge conflicts and renames to non-cloned paths are handled differently in Git, which adds to the cost of switching version control systems.
